task.title: Switching to a different network interface naming scheme

li.p: You have access to the console of the server.

context.p: By default, (RHEL) uses the rhel-9.0 naming scheme, even if you install or update to a later minor version of RHEL. While the default naming scheme fits in most scenarios, there might be reasons to switch to a different scheme version.
Examples when you must change the naming scheme:

li.p: A new scheme can help to better identify a device if it adds additional attributes, such as a slot number, to an interface name.

An new scheme can prevent udev from falling back to the kernel-assigned device names (eth*). This happens if the driver does not provide enough unique attributes for two or more interfaces to generate unique names for them.

info.codeblock: # ip link show 2: eno1: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc fq_codel state UP mode DEFAULT group default qlen 1000 link/ether 00:00:5e:00:53:1a brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff ...
info.p: Record the MAC addresses of the interfaces.

step.cmd: Optional: Display the ID_NET_NAMING_SCHEME property of a network interface to identify the naming scheme that RHEL currently uses:

info.p: Note that the property is not available on the lo loopback device.

step.cmd: Append the net.naming-scheme=<scheme> option to the command line of all installed kernels, for example:
Based on the MAC addresses you recorded, identify the new names of network interfaces that have changed due to the different naming scheme:

info.codeblock: # ip link show 2: eno1np0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 1500 qdisc fq_codel state UP mode DEFAULT group default qlen 1000 link/ether 00:00:5e:00:53:1a brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff ...
info.p: After switching the scheme, udev names the device with the specified MAC address eno1np0, whereas it was named eno1 before.

step.cmd: Identify which NetworkManager connection profile uses an interface with the previous name:

info.codeblock: # nmcli -f device,name connection show DEVICE NAME eno1 example_profile ...

step.cmd: Set the connection.interface-name property in the connection profile to the new interface name:

li.p: Identify the naming scheme that RHEL now uses by displaying the ID_NET_NAMING_SCHEME property of a network interface:
